answer:
**In *Juliana*, the saint physically seizes a demon disguised as an angel who tempts her to sacrifice to idols; she then systematically interrogates it across the ontological divide between human/divine and hellish realms, forcing layered confessions about its mission, origins, tactics, and crimes.**[^1]

A representative moment occurs when Juliana demands the fiend reveal its sender:

> The holy one answered him, through the Spirit’s gift, Juliana: “You must tell me further, fiend of mankind, of your journey, and who sent you to me.” The assailant answered her, seized with fear, despairing of peace: “My father sent me on this errand to you, the king of hell’s inhabitants...”[^2]

She presses onward: “You must tell me further, fiend of souls, how you most harm the righteous through the slide of sin...” extracting vivid admissions of temptation strategies, historical crimes (e.g., inciting Christ's crucifixion, Andrew's martyrdom), and its fear of hell's torments.[^3]

**In *Andreas*, Andrew endures imprisonment among the cannibalistic Mermedonians and confronts their demonic instigators—devils who taunt him across the same ontological gap, boasting of power while he rebukes them into flight via faith and the sign of the cross.**[^4]

A representative moment unfolds when a devil-lord torments the bound saint:

> “What did you hope for, Andreas... Where is your glory...? ...Who is so mighty over middle-earth, that he can release you from your limb-bonds, of all mankind, against my will?” ... To him then Andreas gave this answer: “Behold, the Almighty God can easily save me, the Savior from strife, He who in need long ago fastened you in fiery clamps!”[^5]

Andrew does not elicit confessions but counters with divine authority, shaming the devils' impotence; subordinate fiends attack, recognize Christ's cross on him, and flee terrified.[^6]

**Juliana is the more skilled interrogator**: she dominates through persistent, structured questioning that compels exhaustive self-incrimination, turning the demon into a humiliated witness against hell itself—much like a courtroom cross-examination yielding narrative reversals. Andrew excels at defiant rebuke and expulsion, but his exchanges are reactive taunt-and-rebuke, not probing extraction.[^7]

[^1]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [full chapter](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/).
[^2]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-bd327c](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-bd327c).
[^3]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-def3ab](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-def3ab).
[^4]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [full chapter](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/).
[^5]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-02e4ce](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-02e4ce) to [p-ca8f6e](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca8f6e); [p-1d292e](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-1d292e).
[^6]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-fcbffe](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-fcbffe).
[^7]: Comparative proximity confirmed via wiki cards: *Juliana* ch. 1 nearest to *Andreas* ch. 1 (cosine 0.53); *Andreas* ch. 1 nearest to *Juliana* ch. 1 (cosine 0.53).
